metadata.title: Pointers, References, and Allocation
metadata.description: C, C++, CUDA, and Rust basics around &, *, malloc, cudaMalloc, and references.

questions.stem: In C or C++, if `int x = 10;`, what does the expression `&x` mean?

options.A: The value stored inside `x`
options.B: The memory address of `x`
options.C: A reference variable named `x`

questions.explanation:
B is correct. In C/C++, unary & is the address-of operator. If x is an int, then &x produces the address where that integer object lives.

A is wrong because the value is read by writing x, not &x. C is wrong because &x is an expression, not a new reference declaration.

questions.stem: Given `int x = 10; int* p = &x;`, what does `*p` mean in an expression?

options.A: Declare `p` as a pointer
options.B: Dereference `p` and access the `int` object it points to
options.C: Get the address of the pointer variable `p`

questions.explanation:
B is correct. In a declaration, * helps say p is a pointer. But in an expression like *p, it dereferences the pointer and reaches the pointed-to int.

A confuses declaration syntax with expression syntax. C describes &p, not *p.

questions.stem: Which statement best describes the difference between `int x`, `int* p`, and `int& r` in C++?

options.A: `x` is an integer object, `p` is a pointer that stores an address, and `r` is a reference alias to an existing integer
options.B: `x`, `p`, and `r` all store memory addresses
options.C: `int& r` creates a raw pointer that must be dereferenced with `*r`

questions.explanation:
A is correct. int x is the actual integer object. int* p is a pointer variable whose value is an address of an int. int& r is a reference, which behaves like another name for an existing int.

B is wrong because x stores an integer value, not an address. C is wrong because C++ references are not used with *r.

questions.stem: Why is `int& r = &x;` wrong in C++ when `x` is an `int`?

options.A: Because `&x` has type `int*`, but `int&` must bind to an `int` object
options.B: Because references can only bind to heap memory
options.C: Because `&` is not allowed anywhere in C++ reference code

questions.explanation:
A is correct. A reference like int& r must bind directly to an int, so int& r = x; is valid. But &x is the address of x, so its type is int*, not int.

B is wrong because references can bind to stack objects. C is wrong because & is legal; it is just producing the wrong type here.

questions.stem: Given `int x = 10; int* p = &x;`, which type mapping is correct?

options.A: `&x` is `int*`, `p` is `int*`, `&p` is `int**`, `*p` is `int`, `&*p` is `int*`, and `*&p` is `int*`
options.B: `&x` is `int`, `p` is `int`, `&p` is `int*`, `*p` is `int*`, `&*p` is `int`, and `*&p` is `int`
options.C: `&x`, `p`, `&p`, `*p`, `&*p`, and `*&p` are all `int*`

questions.explanation: A is correct. &x produces a pointer to x, so it is int*. p is already int*. &p is the address of the pointer variable itself, so it is int**. *p reaches the integer, so it is int. &*p goes to the int then takes its address, giving int*. *&p takes the address of p then dereferences back to p, so it is int*.

questions.stem: Given `int x = 10; int* p = &x;`, which statement about `&&x` and `**p` is correct in ordinary C/C++ expression usage?

options.A: `&&x` is just the address of the address of `x`, and `**p` is valid because every pointer can be dereferenced twice
options.B: `&&x` is not the normal way to take address-of twice, and `**p` is invalid because `*p` is an `int`, not another pointer
options.C: `&&x` and `**p` both produce an `int**`

questions.explanation:
B is correct. After *p, you have an int. You cannot dereference that again with another * unless the first dereference produced another pointer. Also, &&x is not a normal address-of-twice expression; in C++ && has separate meanings such as logical AND or rvalue-reference syntax depending on context.

A and C incorrectly treat these symbols as if operators can always be stacked mechanically.

questions.stem: In C, what is the purpose of `malloc(sizeof(int))`?

options.A: Allocate enough heap memory to store one `int` and return the starting address of that memory
options.B: Create an integer initialized to zero on the stack
options.C: Return the integer value stored at the address `sizeof(int)`

questions.explanation:
A is correct. sizeof(int) asks how many bytes one int needs. malloc asks the heap for that many bytes and returns a pointer to the first byte of the block.

B is wrong because malloc allocates heap memory and does not initialize like a normal stack variable. C is wrong because sizeof(int) is a size, not an address to read from.

questions.stem: Why does `malloc` return a pointer instead of returning the allocated object itself?

options.A: Because heap allocation gives you a memory block, and the program needs the block's address to use it later
options.B: Because C functions cannot return integer values
options.C: Because `malloc` always allocates exactly one pointer-sized object

questions.explanation:
A is correct. The heap block lives somewhere in memory. To let your code access and eventually free that block, malloc returns its address.

B is false because C functions can return integers. C is false because malloc allocates the number of bytes you request, not always one pointer-sized object.

questions.stem: Why does CUDA code commonly call `cudaMalloc((void**)&p, sizeof(int))` instead of `p = cudaMalloc(sizeof(int))`?

options.A: Because `cudaMalloc` writes the allocated device address into `p`, so it needs the address of the pointer variable
options.B: Because CUDA device memory can only store `void` values
options.C: Because `cudaMalloc` returns the allocated pointer as its return value

questions.explanation:
A is correct. cudaMalloc uses its return value for an error/status code. The allocated device pointer is returned through an output parameter. To modify your pointer variable p, the function needs &p, which is a pointer to the pointer variable.

B is wrong because void* is a generic pointer type, not a claim that memory stores void values. C describes malloc-style design, not cudaMalloc.

questions.stem: What is the key API-style difference between `malloc` and `cudaMalloc`?

options.A: `malloc` returns the allocated pointer directly, while `cudaMalloc` returns a status code and writes the allocated pointer through a pointer-to-pointer argument
options.B: `malloc` and `cudaMalloc` both return the allocated pointer directly
options.C: `malloc` requires pointer-to-pointer, while `cudaMalloc` does not

questions.explanation:
A is correct. malloc has a return-pointer style: p = malloc(size). cudaMalloc has an output-parameter style: cudaMalloc((void**)&p, size), and the function return value reports success or failure.

B and C reverse or erase the main design difference.

questions.stem: In Rust, what is the best beginner-level description of `&x`?

options.A: A safe reference/borrow of `x`, checked by Rust's borrowing rules
options.B: Exactly the same thing as a C raw pointer in every way
options.C: A request to allocate `x` on the heap

questions.explanation:
A is correct. In Rust, &x creates a reference, also described as borrowing x. It is pointer-like in that it refers to data without owning it, but it is checked by Rust's safety and borrowing rules.

B is wrong because Rust references are not exactly C raw pointers. C is wrong because borrowing does not allocate heap memory.

questions.stem: Which Rust types represent raw pointers?

options.A: `*const T` for a const raw pointer and `*mut T` for a mutable raw pointer
options.B: `&const T` for a const raw pointer and `&mut T` for a mutable raw pointer
options.C: `int*` and `void**`

questions.explanation:
A is correct. Rust raw pointer types use the star syntax: *const T and *mut T. They are distinct from safe references like &T and &mut T.

B mixes reference syntax with raw pointer wording. C is C/C++ style, not Rust type syntax.

questions.stem: Given `int x = 10; int* p = &x;`, what does `&p` represent?

options.A: The address of `x`, with type `int*`
options.B: The address of the pointer variable `p`, with type `int**`
options.C: The integer value stored in `x`, with type `int`

questions.explanation:
B is correct. p is itself a variable. Its value is the address of x, but p also lives somewhere in memory. &p asks for the address of the pointer variable, so its type is int**.

A describes p or &x, not &p. C describes *p.

questions.stem: Which C++ line correctly creates a reference alias `r` for an existing integer `x`?

options.A: `int& r = x;`
options.B: `int& r = &x;`
options.C: `int* r = x;`

questions.explanation:
A is correct. A reference binds to the object itself, so int& r = x; makes r another name for x.

B is wrong because &x is an int*, not an int. C is wrong because an int* needs an address such as &x, not the integer object directly.

questions.stem: In the declaration `int* p = &x;`, what is the best way to read `int*`?

options.A: `p` is a pointer to an `int`
options.B: `p` is an `int` that has already been dereferenced
options.C: `p` is a reference alias to `x`

questions.explanation:
A is correct. In a declaration, int* means pointer-to-int. The variable p stores an address where an int can be found.

B confuses declaration syntax with expression dereference. C describes int&, not int*.

questions.stem: Given `int x = 10; int* p = &x;`, why does `&*p` usually evaluate back to the same address as `p`?

options.A: `*p` reaches the pointed-to `int`, and `&` then takes that same object's address
options.B: `&*p` creates a new pointer variable on the heap
options.C: `&*p` has type `int**` because two symbols are used

questions.explanation:
A is correct. If p points to x, then *p is x. Taking & of that object gives the address of x, which is the value held by p.

B invents allocation that is not happening. C counts symbols instead of tracking types.

questions.stem: Given `int x = 10; int* p = &x;`, what is the type and meaning of `*&p`?

options.A: It is an `int*`, because `&p` gets the address of `p` and `*` dereferences back to `p`
options.B: It is an `int`, because every `*` expression returns an integer
options.C: It is an `int**`, because `&p` can never be simplified

questions.explanation:
A is correct. &p has type int** because it is the address of a pointer variable. Dereferencing it with * gets back the pointer variable p, whose type is int*.

B ignores that dereferencing a pointer-to-pointer gives a pointer. C stops halfway at &p and does not apply the outer *.

questions.stem: Which C snippet correctly stores one heap-allocated integer's address in `p`?

options.A: `int* p = malloc(sizeof(int));`
options.B: `int p = malloc(sizeof(int));`
options.C: `int& p = malloc(sizeof(int));`

questions.explanation:
A is correct. malloc(sizeof(int)) returns a pointer to a block large enough for one int, so the receiving variable should be an int* in C-style code.

B tries to store an address in an integer object. C is C++ reference syntax and does not bind correctly to a heap pointer returned by malloc.

questions.stem: If `int* p;` and a function must change `p` so that it points to newly allocated memory, what kind of argument does that function need?

options.A: The value of `p`, because changing a local copy changes the caller's pointer
options.B: The address of `p`, so the function receives something like an `int**` and can write a new pointer value into `p`
options.C: The dereferenced value `*p`, because allocation changes the integer rather than the pointer

questions.explanation:
B is correct. To change the caller's pointer variable, the function needs access to that variable. Passing &p gives the function the address of the pointer variable, so it can store the newly allocated address into p.

A only changes a copy. C would access the pointed-to integer, not the pointer variable that needs to receive the new address.

questions.stem: Which statement best separates Rust references from Rust raw pointers?

options.A: `&T` and `&mut T` are safe references checked by borrowing rules, while `*const T` and `*mut T` are raw pointer types
options.B: `&T` is always unsafe, while `*const T` is always safe
options.C: Rust has references but no raw pointer types

questions.explanation:
A is correct. Rust references such as &T and &mut T participate in borrow checking. Raw pointers use *const T and *mut T and are the lower-level pointer forms.

B reverses the safety intuition. C is wrong because Rust does have raw pointer types.

questions.stem: Given `int x = 7; int* p = &x; int& r = x;`, which statement is correct?

options.A: `r` is an alias for `x`, `p` stores the address of `x`, and `*p` accesses the same integer object as `r`
options.B: `r` stores the address of `x`, so `*r` is required to read the integer
options.C: `p` and `r` are both raw pointers with type `int*`

questions.explanation:
A is correct. r is a C++ reference alias for x. p stores &x. Dereferencing p with *p reaches the same integer object that r names directly.

B incorrectly treats a reference as a pointer. C erases the difference between pointer and reference types.
